Intro1: Working memory allows us to hold information in our minds long enough to use it. It plays a central role in everyday thinking, such as following instructions or keeping track of a task as it unfolds. Rather than acting as a simple storage space, short-term memory helps maintain and update information as we use it. Because of this, it supports reasoning and decision-making across many contexts (Baddeley, 2003; Cowan, 2010).
Intro2: AI tools can reduce how often you have to keep details active in your head. Things like summaries and reminders make it easy to offload information as soon as it becomes inconvenient. That support is useful, but solving difficult problems often involves combining lots of pieces of information at the same time. AI reliance may therefore limit our abilities to perform this sort of analytical thinking.
Practice1: Research suggests that short-term memory is not fixed. Practice that requires briefly holding and using information can improve performance on related tasks (Klingberg, 2010; Shipstead, Redick, & Engle, 2012). Well-designed exercises challenge this system in manageable ways. They encourage active maintenance rather than simple repetition.
Practice2: These exercises are not about memorizing facts or pushing capacity to its limits. The goal is to strengthen the ability to keep information available long enough to work with it. By practicing short, focused memory tasks, you are supporting a cognitive function that underlies clear thinking in many areas of life.
Practice3: To get the most out of these exercises, read the passage slowly, and only once.
Ref_Baddeley: Baddeley, A. (2003). Working memory: Looking back and looking forward. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 4(10), 829–839.
Ref_Cowan: Cowan, N. (2010). The magical mystery four: How is working memory capacity limited, and why? Current Directions in Psychological Science, 19(1), 51–57.
Ref_Klingberg: Klingberg, T. (2010). Training and plasticity of working memory. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 14(7), 317–324.
Ref_Shipstead: Shipstead, Z., Redick, T. S., & Engle, R. W. (2012). Is working memory training effective? Psychological Bulletin, 138(4), 628–654.
